--- SOURCE 5: Plasmonic metal nanostructures: concepts, challenges and ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792

SUMMARY:
Apart from the photothermal effects and energetic charge carriers, plasmon-induced potential was proposed to be responsible for the enhancement of reaction kinetics at illuminated PMNs in newly published studies (Wilson et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Wilson and Jain, 2020). Alongside a growingly large number of studies on modifying chemical transformations by LSPs of PMNs, there is an urgent need for an accurate and deep mechanistic understanding of the observed plasmonic enhancement in PMCRs. It has been demonstrated that the plasmonic enhancements of PMCRs are intimately related to the confined intense electromagnetic fields, energetic charge carriers, and photothermal effects in optically excited PMNs. For example, the intense electromagnetic fields at the surface of illuminated Au NPs facilitate the polymerization of SU-8 that is a triarylium-sulfonium salt-based photo-initiator (Ueno et al., 2008).

Perspective

Plasmonic metal nanostructures: concepts, challenges and opportunities in photo-mediated chemical transformations
==============================================================================================================================

Summary
-------

Plasmonic metal [nanostructures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanostructures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PMNs) are characterized by the [plasmon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/plasmon "Learn more about plasmon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") oscillation of [conduction band](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/conduction-band "Learn more about conduction band from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") electron in response to external radiation, enabling strong light absorption and scattering capacities and near-field amplification. Owing to these enhanced light-matter interactions, PMNs have garnered extensive research interest in the past decades. Notably, a growingly large number of reports show that the [energetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and kinetics of chemical transformations on PMNs can be modified upon photoexcitation of their plasmons, giving rise to a new paradigm of manipulating the reaction rate and selectivity of chemical reactions. On the other hand, there is urgent need to achieve clear understanding of the mechanism underlying the photo-mediated chemical transformations on PMNs for unleashing their full potential in converting solar energy to chemicals. In this perspective, we review current fundamental concepts of photo-mediated chemical transformations executed at PMNs. Three pivotal mechanistic questions, i.e., thermal and nonthermal effects, direct and indirect charge transfer processes, and the specific impacts of plasmon-induced potentials, are explored based on recent studies. We highlight the critical aspects in which major advancements should be made to facilitate the rational design and optimization of photo-mediated chemical transformations on PMNs in the future.

Subject areas
-------------

Chemical Reaction Engineering

Nanomaterials

Nanostructure

Introduction
------------

Plasmonic metal [nanostructures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanostructures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PMNs) have garnered broad research interests owing to their strong interactions with incident light through localized [surface plasmons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/surface-plasmon "Learn more about surface plasmons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LSPs) that are the collective oscillation of [free electrons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/free-electron "Learn more about free electrons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") under light excitation ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig1)A). When the frequency of electron oscillation matches that of the incident light, [localized surface plasmon resonance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/localized-surface-plasmon-resonance "Learn more about localized surface plasmon resonance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LSPR) is established. Under the resonant conditions, PMNs display substantially larger optical extinction (absorption and scattering) cross-sections than their physical ones ([Ming et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib38)), along with remarkably intense local electric fields (|_E_|2, compared with the incident field, [Figures 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig1)B and 1C) ([Jiang et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib23)). Following the excitation ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig1)D), LSPs are relaxed by either re-emitting photons to free space (radiative decay) or yielding [energetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") charge carriers and heating up the [crystal lattice](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/crystal-lattice "Learn more about crystal lattice from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (nonradiative decays) ([Linic et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30)). By virtue of these unique [optical properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/optical-property "Learn more about optical properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), PMNs have been used in a wide range of applications, such as field-enhanced spectroscopy ([Willets and Van Duyne, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib69)), photodetection ([Knight et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib26)), [photovoltaics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photovoltaics "Learn more about photovoltaics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Atwater and Polman, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib2)), imaging ([Boyer et al., 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib9)), sensing ([Liu et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib34)), photothermal therapy ([Ding et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib17)), and so on.

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004220311792-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (538KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004220311792-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (538KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004220311792-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Figure 1. LSPR excitation and decay in PMNs, as well as their optical features

(A) Schematic illustration of collective oscillation of conduction-band electrons in PMNs.

(B) The extinction cross section versus the physical cross section of various nanoscale optical species ([Ming et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib38)).

(C) Logarithmic-scale electric field intensity amplification contours of differently shaped Au nanocrystals ([Jiang et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib23)).

(D) Radiative and nonradiative decay processes of LSPs in PMNs ([Linic et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30)).

In recent years, [plasmon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/plasmon "Learn more about plasmon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") excitation of PMNs has been proved to be able to modify energetics and kinetics of chemical transformations, leading to more desirable reaction rate and/or product distribution at milder conditions than conventional thermal catalysis ([Linic et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib29), [2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30); [Brongersma et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib10); [Zhan et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib77); [Kale et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib24); [Corson et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib15)). This burgeoning research field is commonly termed plasmon-mediated chemical reactions (PMCRs). PMCRs not only open up a new avenue to manipulate the reaction outcomes, which is otherwise difficult/unfeasible in dark conditions but also provide an efficient means for harvesting and converting solar energy through funneling the [light energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/optical-power "Learn more about light energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") into chemical bonds ([Linic et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30); [Brongersma et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib10); [Li et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib32)). To optimize the performance of PMCRs, their underlying mechanisms must be fully understood, which is under intensive scientific studies.

PMCRs are essentially heterogeneous catalytic processes involving multiple complicated interactions of three bodies: photons, PMNs, and reaction molecules. It is generally regarded that PMNs concentrate the energy of incoming photons in small volumes through their plasmonic oscillation of [conduction electrons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/conduction-electron "Learn more about conduction electrons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Photothermal effects and energetic charge carriers from nonradiative plasmon decay are accounted for the observed desirable reaction results at illuminated PMNs. However, the individual contributions of photothermal effects and energetic charge carriers in PMCRs are still under vigorous debate ([Zhou et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib85), [2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib86); [Sivan et al., 2019a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib57), [2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib59); [Baffou et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib6); [Dubi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib18); [Jain et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib91); [Sivan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib59)). In addition, the specific mechanism by which energetic charge carriers interact with molecules that undergo chemical reactions needs to be further elucidated ([Kale et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib24); [Brongersma et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib10); [Mubeen et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib39)). Apart from the photothermal effects and energetic charge carriers, plasmon-induced potential was proposed to be responsible for the enhancement of reaction kinetics at illuminated PMNs in newly published studies ([Wilson et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib71); [Wang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib67); [Wilson and Jain, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib70)). Herein, we give a brief introduction of pioneering and representative works about PMCRs and focus on three major aspects of the mechanism explanation, i.e., thermal and nonthermal effects, direct and indirect charge transfer, and plasmon-induced potentials. Critical viewpoints and perspectives on these issues are provided, in the hope of shedding some light on the mechanistic understanding and their industrial promises of PMCRs.

Plasmon-mediated chemical reactions
-----------------------------------

PMCRs feature a distinct reaction rate and/or product distribution at a milder reaction condition at the illuminated [PMNs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanomaterial "Learn more about PMNs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") than the conventional thermal reactions. In 2013, Mukherjee et al. demonstrated the enhancement of dissociation rate of H 2 at Au [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (NPs) supported on the TiO 2 matrix under illumination ([Mukherjee et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib40)). By detecting the formation of HD molecules from the dissociations of H 2 and D 2, an almost 6-fold increase was identified ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)A), which can be further improved by replacing TiO 2 with SiO 2 ([Mukherjee et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib41)). Illuminated nanostructured Ag was observed to accelerate [catalytic oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/catalytic-oxidation "Learn more about catalytic oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reactions, such as ethylene [epoxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/epoxidation "Learn more about epoxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), CO [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and NH 3 oxidation ([Christopher et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib13), [2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib14)). The steady-state reaction rate of ethylene [epoxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/epoxidation "Learn more about epoxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was increased about 4-fold with visible illumination at 250 mW/cm 2 compared with that of the thermal process ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)B). A recent publication revealed that excited plasmon remarkably facilitated ammonia decomposition at plasmonic antenna-reactor [photocatalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photocatalysts "Learn more about photocatalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that consisted of a Cu antenna and Ru reactor sites ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)C) ([Zhou et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib85)).

![Image 13](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004220311792-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (804KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004220311792-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (804KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004220311792-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Figure 2. Photo-mediated chemical transformations on PMNs

(A) Real-time dissociation rate of H 2/D 2 on Au/TiO 2[photocatalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photocatalysts "Learn more about photocatalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with laser (2.41 W/cm 2) intermittently switched on/off ([Mukherjee et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib40)).

(B) The rate of ethylene epoxidation on plasmonic Ag with/without 250 mW/cm 2 visible light illumination ([Christopher et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib13)).

(C) Real-time rate of NH 3 decomposition on plasmonic antenna-reactor photocatalyst consisting of a Cu NP antenna and Ru reactor sites with 9.6 W/cm 2 white light switched on/off ([Zhou et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib85)).

(D) Selectivity to [propylene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/propylene "Learn more about propylene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") epoxidation (PO) as a function of the reaction rate on Cu NPs with illumination by visible light at an intensity of 550 mW/cm 2 and in dark conditions ([Marimuthu et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib36)).

(E) Production rates of CH 4 and CO on Rh/Al 2 O 3 (left) and Au/Al 2 O 3 (right) with UV illumination at an intensity of 3W/cm 2 on/off ([Zhang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib79)).

(F) The regioselective cleavage of unsymmetric [iodonium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/iodonium "Learn more about iodonium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") salts on plasmonic Au ([Miliutina et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib37)).

In addition to achieving a desirable reaction rate, excited [LSPs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/surface-plasmon "Learn more about LSPs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are able to manipulate product selectivity. The steady-state selectivity in [propylene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/propene "Learn more about propylene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") epoxidation at the surface of Cu NPs was considerably increased upon photoexcitation of Cu [LSPs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/surface-plasmon "Learn more about LSPs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)D) ([Marimuthu et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib36)). Excited LSPs of Al were proved effective for selective [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") conversion to [carbon monoxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about carbon monoxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the reverse water-gas shift reaction ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)E) ([Robatjazi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib50)). Postnikov et al. even demonstrated the probability of manipulating [regioselectivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/regioselectivity "Learn more about regioselectivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in cleavage reaction of unsymmetric [iodonium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/iodonium "Learn more about iodonium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") salts by Au LSPs, opening a novel avenue for the fine control of reactivity in [organic chemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/organic-chemistry "Learn more about organic chemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)F) ([Miliutina et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib37)). Some other promising applications were reviewed in several excellent articles ([Linic et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib29), [2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30); [Kale et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib24); [Brongersma et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib10); [Li et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib32); [Zhang et al., 2018a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib80)).

Alongside a growingly large number of studies on modifying chemical transformations by LSPs of [PMNs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocrystalline-material "Learn more about PMNs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), there is an urgent need for an accurate and deep mechanistic understanding of the observed plasmonic enhancement in PMCRs. It has been demonstrated that the plasmonic enhancements of PMCRs are intimately related to the confined intense [electromagnetic fields](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electromagnetic-field "Learn more about electromagnetic fields from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [energetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") charge carriers, and photothermal effects in optically excited PMNs. For example, the intense electromagnetic fields at the surface of illuminated Au NPs facilitate the polymerization of SU-8 that is a triarylium-sulfonium salt-based photo-initiator ([Ueno et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib63)). The photothermal effects promote the decomposition of dicumyl [peroxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/peroxide "Learn more about peroxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to 2-phenyl-2-propanol and [acetophenone](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/acetophenone "Learn more about acetophenone from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on illuminated Au NPs ([Fasciani et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib20)). Energetic charge carriers enhance [carbon dioxide reduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-dioxide-reduction "Learn more about carbon dioxide reduction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), O 2 dissociation and oxidation reactions, H 2 dissociation and hydrogenation reactions, N 2 fixation and NH 3 decomposition, and CO 2 reduction and/or dissociation on PMNs ([Li et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib32)). More encouragingly, some studies at single particle and single turnover event level provide invaluable insights into the fundamental processes of PMCRs, e.g., the rate-determining step and its [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/reaction-activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the spatial distribution of reaction sites ([Zhao and Chen, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib82); [Tachikawa et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib61)). By using single-molecule fluorescence, the plasmon enhanced-fluorogenic oxidation of Amplex Red by H 2 O 2 on a single Au [nanorod](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanorod "Learn more about nanorod from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was investigated with sub-turnover resolution ([Li et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib33)). The activation energies of intermediate generation, product generation, and product desorption were clearly differentiated, showing that the excited LSPs mainly accelerate the intermediate generation (rate-limiting step) by substantially lowering its [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Dionne et al. demonstrated that LSPs drive reaction nucleation at specific sites closest to the electromagnetic hot spots in the [dehydrogenation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/dehydrogenation "Learn more about dehydrogenation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of Pd nanocubes adjacent to an Au [nanodisc](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanodisc "Learn more about nanodisc from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Page

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Plasmonic nanostructures enable collective oscillations of conduction electrons in response to incident light, enhancing light-matter interactions and creating localized electromagnetic fields.
SPR occurs when incident light matches the natural frequency of collective electron oscillations, resulting in significant absorption and scattering of light, crucial for sensing applications.
LSPR refers to the confinement of surface plasmons to metallic nanoparticles, leading to enhanced local electric fields and stronger optical responses, particularly in nanoscale devices.

enriched_points.point_title: Tailored optical responses based on geometry
enriched_points.expanded_content: The geometry of plasmonic nanostructures can be engineered to achieve specific optical responses, enabling applications in sensors, photothermal therapy, and photonic devices.
